verses.text: And Hiram king of Tyre sent his servants unto Solomon; for he had heard that they had anointed him king in the room of his father: for Hiram was ever a lover of David. Guidance: The chapter opens with diplomatic recognition. Hiram I of Tyre (reigning roughly 980-947 BC) was a long-time ally of David and the Phoenician supplier of cedar and craftsmen for David's Jerusalem palace (2 Sam 5:11). On hearing of Solomon's accession, he sends an embassy — the standard Near-Eastern protocol of acknowledging a new monarch in a friendly state. The narrator's note is touching: 'Hiram was ever a lover of David' (ki ohev hayah Chiram le-David — for Hiram had always been a lover/friend to David). The Hebrew ohev — lover — in royal diplomatic vocabulary is the technical term for treaty-partner; but here it carries personal warmth. David and Hiram had been allies for decades; they were friends. Hiram's diplomatic warmth toward Solomon is grounded in personal affection for his father. The chapter is showing us that the relationships David built across decades pay forward into Solomon's reign. The temple project — which Solomon will now propose — would not have been logistically possible without this preexisting friendship. The LORD's purposes are often advanced through human bonds carefully cultivated by previous generations. The chapter is teaching us a quiet principle: today's projects often rest on yesterday's faithful relationships. The temple gets built because David, decades earlier, was the kind of man Hiram remained loyal to.

And Solomon sent to Hiram, saying, Thou knowest how that David my father could not build an house unto the name of the LORD his God for the wars which were about him on every side, until the LORD put them under the soles of his feet. But now the LORD my God hath given me rest on every side, so that there is neither adversary nor evil occurrent. And, behold, I purpose to build an house unto the name of the LORD my God, as the LORD spake unto David my father, saying, Thy son, whom I will set upon thy throne in thy room, he shall build an house unto my name. Now therefore command thou that they hew me cedar trees out of Lebanon; and my servants shall be with thy servants: and unto thee will I give hire for thy servants according to all that thou shalt appoint: for thou knowest that there is not among us any that can skill to hew timber like unto the Sidonians. Guidance: Solomon's letter to Hiram — a model of diplomatic-theological correspondence. The structure is precise. (1) Historical framing: 'David my father could not build an house unto the name of the LORD his God for the wars which were about him on every side.' Solomon recounts the situation. David wanted to build the temple but was prevented by constant warfare. The Hebrew 'unto the name of the LORD' (le-shem YHWH) is the deuteronomic phrase for the central sanctuary (Deut 12:5) — the place where the LORD's name dwells. (2) The change in circumstances: 'Now the LORD my God hath given me rest on every side, so that there is neither adversary nor evil occurrent.' Solomon contrasts his peace with David's wars. The Hebrew satan (adversary) and pega ra (evil occurrence) — no enemy, no incident. The era of war has ended; the era of building has begun. (3) The theological warrant: 'The LORD spake unto David my father, saying, Thy son… he shall build an house unto my name.' Solomon cites the Davidic Covenant of 2 Sam 7:13 directly. The temple project is not Solomon's idea; it is the fulfillment of a prophecy given to David thirty years earlier. (4) The specific request: 'Command thou that they hew me cedar trees out of Lebanon.' Lebanon cedars — the most famous timber of the ancient Near East, exported for royal construction across Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the eastern Mediterranean. (5) The financial terms: 'My servants shall be with thy servants: and unto thee will I give hire.' Joint workforces; Solomon pays full wages. (6) The professional acknowledgment: 'There is not among us any that can skill to hew timber like unto the Sidonians.' Hebrew yode'a likhrot etzim — those who know how to cut trees. Solomon humbly concedes Israelite inferiority in the specialized craft of working cedar timber. The chapter is showing us a king who can flatter his trading partner while still presenting his project as the LORD's work. Solomon's diplomacy is theologically grounded and humanly intelligent — both at once.

And it came to pass, when Hiram heard the words of Solomon, that he rejoiced greatly, and said, Blessed be the LORD this day, which hath given unto David a wise son over this great people. Guidance: Hiram's response — striking on two levels. (1) Politically: he rejoices. The arrangement is excellent business for Tyre — Phoenicia had abundant cedar and skilled timbermen but limited agricultural land; Israel had plentiful wheat and oil but no cedar forests. The trade is naturally complementary. Hiram's rejoicing reflects shrewd commercial calculation: the deal will supply Tyre with grain and oil for years. (2) Theologically: Hiram, a Phoenician king with his own pantheon (chief god Melqart), blesses the LORD (YHWH). 'Blessed be the LORD this day' — baruch YHWH ha-yom. The Phoenician king names the Israelite God by His covenant name and recognizes that this God has given David a wise son. The chapter is recording a remarkable moment of foreign theological acknowledgment. Hiram is not converting to Israelite monotheism; he is doing the standard ancient-Near-Eastern courtesy of recognizing the deity of one's treaty partner. But the language is striking. Solomon's wisdom has not only made an internal impression in Israel; it has reached the Phoenician court and produced this verbal honor to the LORD. The chapter is teaching us that the wisdom granted by God to one of His servants can produce, even in pagan observers, momentary acknowledgments that the LORD deserves blessing. The principle will reappear when the Queen of Sheba similarly blesses the LORD on encountering Solomon's wisdom (1 Kgs 10:9). The chapter is showing us a small foreshadowing of the larger biblical hope: the nations recognizing the God of Israel through the wisdom and works of His anointed king.

And Hiram sent to Solomon, saying, I have considered the things which thou sentest to me for: and I will do all thy desire concerning timber of cedar, and concerning timber of fir. My servants shall bring them down from Lebanon unto the sea: and I will convey them by sea in floats unto the place that thou shalt appoint me, and will cause them to be discharged there, and thou shalt receive them: and thou shalt accomplish my desire, in giving food for my household. So Hiram gave Solomon cedar trees and fir trees according to all his desire. And Solomon gave Hiram twenty thousand measures of wheat for food to his household, and twenty measures of pure oil: thus gave Solomon to Hiram year by year. Guidance: The contract terms in operational detail. Hiram offers both cedar (erez — the prestige timber) and fir/cypress (berosh — the secondary structural wood). He proposes a clever logistical solution: 'My servants shall bring them down from Lebanon unto the sea: and I will convey them by sea in floats.' The Phoenicians, sea-going experts, would float bundled cedar logs down the Mediterranean coast from the Lebanon ports (probably Sidon or Byblos) to Joppa — the Israelite coastal access point — where Solomon's men would receive and transport them inland. The image is vivid: rafts of cedar floating along the coastline for weeks, then dragged ashore at Joppa and hauled overland up the Jerusalem road. The payment: 20,000 kor of wheat ('measures' — about 130,000 bushels) and (the Hebrew text has) 20 kor of pure oil ('measures' — possibly a copying problem; the parallel in 2 Chr 2:10 has 20,000 baths of oil, which fits better with the wheat quantity). The shipment was annual: 'year by year' (shanah be-shanah). The chapter is showing us a major international commerce relationship: each year, Israel's surplus wheat and olive oil flowed north to Phoenicia; each year, Phoenicia's cedar timber and craftsmanship flowed south. The trade was the practical underpinning of the temple project. The chapter is honest that even the LORD's temple required logistics, contracts, supply chains, and skilled foreign labor. The kingdom of God works through normal economic exchanges, not by avoiding them.

And the LORD gave Solomon wisdom, as he promised him: and there was peace between Hiram and Solomon; and they two made a league together. Guidance: The theological summary. 'The LORD gave Solomon wisdom, as he promised him.' The Gibeon-dream promise of chapter 3 is here invoked as the explanation for Solomon's deft handling of the Hiram negotiation. The wisdom is operational, not just judicial — it makes Solomon an effective international diplomat as well as a wise judge. 'There was peace between Hiram and Solomon; and they two made a league together.' The Hebrew vayikhrethu berith — they cut a covenant. The same word used for the Mosaic covenant, the Davidic covenant — applied here to an international treaty. The Solomon-Hiram alliance is formalized; the agreement is sealed with the standard ceremonial covenant-making. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD's gift of wisdom equips Solomon for the whole range of royal responsibilities, including foreign affairs. The judgment of two harlots in chapter 3 and the timber-deal with Hiram in chapter 5 are both products of the same divine endowment. The chapter is also showing us a peaceful international order — the kind that prophets like Isaiah will later anticipate for the messianic age, when 'they shall beat their swords into plowshares' (Isa 2:4). The Solomon-Hiram covenant is, in miniature, a foretaste of the world-peace that the LORD ultimately intends. Wise kings negotiating fair trade agreements that benefit both peoples — this is part of what the LORD's reign through His anointed looks like.

And king Solomon raised a levy out of all Israel; and the levy was thirty thousand men. And he sent them to Lebanon, ten thousand a month by courses: a month they were in Lebanon, and two months at home: and Adoniram was over the levy. Guidance: The Israelite labor draft. Solomon raises a mas — the technical Hebrew term for state-conscripted labor (corvée). The same word the Egyptians used for the Hebrew slavery (Exod 1:11) — a charged word with associations of forced servitude. The number: 30,000 men. The rotation: 10,000 a month, one month in Lebanon plus two months at home. So each conscript served four months a year — a third of the year on the king's project, two-thirds at home with his family and farm. Adoniram (the same officer of 1 Kgs 4:6) was the corvée master. The chapter is reporting this neutrally, but the deuteronomic warning in Deut 17:14-20 had urged caution about kings building large administrative-military apparatuses. The corvée labor that Solomon raised for the temple would later become deeply unpopular — the very same Adoniram would be stoned to death by Israelites when Rehoboam tried to maintain the system (1 Kgs 12:18). The chapter is preserving the operational detail of how the temple actually got built: not by paid voluntary labor but by rotating state conscription. The Israelite men of working age took one-month tours of duty in the Lebanon mountains as a tax-in-labor obligation. The chapter is not romanticizing the temple project; it is honest about the human cost. Even building the LORD's house involved a system of state-coerced labor that contained the seeds of the later kingdom split.

And Solomon had threescore and ten thousand that bare burdens, and fourscore thousand hewers in the mountains; Beside the chief of Solomon's officers which were over the work, three thousand and three hundred, which ruled over the people that wrought in the work. Guidance: The full labor force tally. Three categories. (1) 70,000 'that bare burdens' (nose sabbal) — load-carriers; men assigned to transport stone, timber, and other materials. (2) 80,000 'hewers in the mountains' (chotzev ba-har) — stone-cutters working the quarries. The chapter is showing us that the temple required massive quantities of cut limestone, the dominant building material of the Jerusalem highlands. The quarrying alone occupied a workforce the size of a small modern city. (3) 3,300 supervisors — 'chief of Solomon's officers… which ruled over the people.' (The parallel in 2 Chr 2:18 has 3,600; both numbers may be subdivisions of the same total.) The supervisory ratio is roughly 1 supervisor per 50 workers — comparable to ancient Egyptian construction projects. Total workforce when fully mobilized: 30,000 conscripted Israelites + 70,000 burden-bearers + 80,000 stone-cutters + 3,300 foremen = around 183,000 men. This is a national-scale public-works project. According to 2 Chr 2:17, the 150,000 burden-bearers and stone-cutters were drawn from the non-Israelite resident-aliens (gerim) — descendants of the original Canaanite populations who remained in the land. So the heaviest physical labor fell on the foreign residents; the rotating Israelite conscription was the lighter and more dignified service. The chapter is recording the social structure of Solomon's economy: a top tier of free Israelites who served by rotation; a middle tier of supervisors; a bottom tier of non-Israelite laborers who did the back-breaking quarrying. The kingdom of Solomon is built on a tiered labor system that — by modern moral standards — sits uncomfortably. The chapter is honest about this.

And Solomon had threescore and ten thousand that bare burdens, and fourscore thousand hewers in the mountains; Beside the chief of Solomon's officers which were over the work, three thousand and three hundred, which ruled over the people that wrought in the work. Guidance: The full labor force tally. Three categories. (1) 70,000 'that bare burdens' (nose sabbal) — load-carriers; men assigned to transport stone, timber, and other materials. (2) 80,000 'hewers in the mountains' (chotzev ba-har) — stone-cutters working the quarries. The chapter is showing us that the temple required massive quantities of cut limestone, the dominant building material of the Jerusalem highlands. The quarrying alone occupied a workforce the size of a small modern city. (3) 3,300 supervisors — 'chief of Solomon's officers… which ruled over the people.' (The parallel in 2 Chr 2:18 has 3,600; both numbers may be subdivisions of the same total.) The supervisory ratio is roughly 1 supervisor per 50 workers — comparable to ancient Egyptian construction projects. Total workforce when fully mobilized: 30,000 conscripted Israelites + 70,000 burden-bearers + 80,000 stone-cutters + 3,300 foremen = around 183,000 men. This is a national-scale public-works project. According to 2 Chr 2:17, the 150,000 burden-bearers and stone-cutters were drawn from the non-Israelite resident-aliens (gerim) — descendants of the original Canaanite populations who remained in the land. So the heaviest physical labor fell on the foreign residents; the rotating Israelite conscription was the lighter and more dignified service. The chapter is recording the social structure of Solomon's economy: a top tier of free Israelites who served by rotation; a middle tier of supervisors; a bottom tier of non-Israelite laborers who did the back-breaking quarrying. The kingdom of Solomon is built on a tiered labor system that — by modern moral standards — sits uncomfortably. The chapter is honest about this.

And the king commanded, and they brought great stones, costly stones, and hewed stones, to lay the foundation of the house. And Solomon's builders and Hiram's builders did hew them, and the stonesquarers: so they built the house. Guidance: The foundation work begins. 'Great stones, costly stones, and hewed stones' (avanim gedolot, avanim yeqarot, avnei gazit — large stones, expensive stones, dressed/cut stones). The Hebrew gazit refers to ashlar — stones cut to precise dimensions for fitted masonry, the high-quality construction technique that distinguishes royal buildings in the ancient world. Some of these massive foundation stones can still be seen on the Temple Mount today (the Western Wall preserves stones from Herod's later expansion built on the original Solomonic foundations). 'Solomon's builders and Hiram's builders did hew them.' The Israelite and Phoenician craftsmen worked together; the temple was a multinational construction project. 'The stonesquarers' — Hebrew ha-Givlim — 'the men of Gebal,' referring to the Phoenician city of Byblos (modern Jbail in Lebanon), famous for its master masons. The Gibelites/Gebalites were the most expert stone-cutters in the eastern Mediterranean; Solomon used them for the precision masonry. The chapter is showing us that the temple's foundation involved deep collaboration with non-Israelite craftsmen. The LORD's house was being built with foreign expertise and foreign labor, on the foundation of David's political alliances, with Solomon's wisdom organizing the whole. The temple is, in its origin, both deeply Israelite (built for the LORD's name) and richly international (using cedars, craftsmen, and trade partners from across the region). The chapter is ending where it began — with international cooperation as the means by which the LORD's house is rising. The chapter is preparing us for chapter 6, where the actual building of the structure begins. The foundation stones are now in place; the cedars are arriving by sea; the workforce is mobilized. Everything is ready for construction to commence.
